appreciate it.

Of course.

It just makes it super easy when people join one.

Three, four thousand people that would just do that and then that plus like LinkedIn been getting slightly better at my LinkedIn game, you know, over the last year.
But, you know, you know, or you guys know the game, you know, early stage startups, you gotta do what you gotta do to not just dump money in ads, you know.

Yeah.
Perfect.
Awesome.

That's a chat today and learn more about how we could partner together and how GrowthX can continue to accelerate our growth.

Definitely more than that.
That's what I'm not going to ask.
All right.
All right.
And my name is Don.

I've been at Alex for, this is my fourth week now as like being the Growth Initiative and just trying to figure out what are the channels that we should be investing right now and that are going to pay off right now.
And then also as well, the channels in the longer term that we should be starting to invest in right now.
So that we can really build a strong growth engine that, you know, a year from now is just going to be generating a ton of SQLs.
Nice.

So I've been leading growth marketing and building content programs at brands for 15 years.
So the main thing when I was at IBM early in my career was just realizing that all these brands were just dumping so much money in ads and things that just don't scale.
And I was like, why don't we just buy a publication in our space that's actually cheaper than even ads?
And so I set out to kind of prove that model out by just building publications for brands.

And I started out with IBM, I built a site called securityintelligence.com, and HB, I built a site called techbeacon.com, I built a contributor network, grew the site to a million visitors a month, and it was all organic, never using any paid.
And just kept doing that, and then it was that leading marketing and growth at different startups that ended up doing pretty decent.

So HashiCorp, went from six to a hundred million when I was there, eventually went public, Service Titan, eventually went public, I built the entire content program, hired their now CMO, Brian, their head of content, built a whole program there, Scale AI, we went from a hundred to three hundred million while I was there.

I was their first and only CMO for two years, you know, you saw all the meta stuff, but yeah, and then I was at DeepGram, and that's when I started kind of building these workflows to streamline a lot of the things I had done in the past, and then hiring people in the loop, and I think that was a big unlock was realize.
I a lot of people were just trying to automate things.
And for me, I wasn't trying to automate.
I was trying more to augment what worked for me and to kind of help people that I would hire anyways to be 10 times more productive.
And I think that's just a different approach.
We're not trying to hit a button and automate publishing that's garbage.
We're trying to take what works for each company and trying to say, hey, can AI help 10x this part of the process and improve the quality of this other part of the process?
And double-check that no one's making a mistake over here, as opposed to saying like, hey, let's remove humans from the equation altogether, you know?
So that's kind of like our approach, which is a little bit, I think, slightly different.
But yeah, and then quickly on the company, we started Bootstrap Profitable and then started growing a lot earlier this year.
And so raised to Series A, went straight to Series A, $12 million, and we're now on path.
$250 million in revenue this year, and we're 70 employees, and serving a little over 50 customers, which has been awesome.
So a lot of really cool, cool companies we get to work with, you know, in a lot of different, different spaces.
So, yeah, and then Tyler got you beaten on his, it's his second week, not fourth, you know, so.
Yeah, great, great to meet the team.
Just quick intro, started my career in strategy consulting, been in enterprise software.
Particularly content marketing for the last seven years, and just love what Marcel has done with, you know, the quality of services, speed of AI, and scale of software.
So teams don't have to spend all this time, right, like learning how to use tools.
We can be that end-to-end partner from strategy to publishing.
Ah, perfect.

By now in this current stage, you know, especially as you did a major upgrade on the brand and name and, you know, and like, can you tell us a little bit more about the audiences you guys are going after right now?

So it's like a pretty large market.

And so these are really our two.
main ICPs today.

Is it sales led?

I guess there's some intent there.

Yeah.

guess for me is like breaking it down from, uh, maybe like a first principles perspective on exactly, um, how we go from, you know, however much we would pay for this, whatever it is to like something meaningful, um, if that, if you think is going through the product or, you know, what have you, I think that's, that would be really helpful.

The TLDR is like, I think, remember when socials started getting popular and stuff and people were just like, almost like websites are not going to exist.
It's all social, you know.
It kind of feels like we're in a similar moment and we personally don't believe that websites are going to go away or, you know, social is going to stop being relevant.
It's just that the fact that right now clicks are disappearing a lot faster and it's just a different vector.

But this vector is actually like very, very meaningful because whereas before maybe people are searching and spending 30 seconds on a bunch of  pages, you know, now they're maybe spending 20 minutes back and forth with an LLN, right?
And so by the time, if you show up, by the time they do start to trust your brand.
Because of those interactions and that information that they're consuming, then by the time they show up, the intent is much higher.

We actually have a customer that I mentioned later that is seen that where, like, any of the leads that come in from LLM referrals, for example, are converting at, like, three to five X higher rate, you know.

And so, and then, like, there's also just a lot of factors here that are, the way we kind of approach this, if you think about it, taking a step back, is, like, we're not ever doing anything when it comes to content and growth that would be detrimental to your audience, even if it means that LLMs are rewarding you today, right?

Like, and so a lot of what we focus on in our process that I'll show you is, like, how do we find the things that, yes, sure, LLMs are rewarding right now, but they're also the right thing to do for your audience, right?
And so kind of how we approach this whole thing from first principles is taking this kind of audience-first approach to content, right?
And so, like, specifically...
And our process, what we do.
I'm flying through because I'll show you actual examples.

And then, like, how do we really measure ROI would be great.

Like, you cannot scale really, really high quality content if you don't have a strong process of generating these artifacts or context engineering, right?

Like, and so a lot of that comes from us having a deeper understanding not only of your company and your product and what you do, but also your audience, whether that's interviews, whether it's research and everything else.
And then we create these artifacts that I'll show you.

They have this context aware engine that is, they're able to use where they are kind of playing with it, you know, like, so, and then as we're editing and creating content for you and we're learning new things, we can come back in here and refresh these artifacts on a regular basis, you know?
And so that's the context engine, if you will.
And then from there, we're creating an entire content OS of all the opportunities you want to go after.
So I'll skip that for a second, but just know that there's a lot of these opportunity identification that's mainly based on, and I'll just show you a visual, us mapping out your audience, right?
Everything they care about, their skills, their background, career trajectory, jobs to be done, pain points, all of that.
And then mapping the topics that align to that.
So think of it as creating a knowledge graph for your audience, right?

And then we're going and doing a ton of research.
And the part that's really interesting with this research, it injects...
That's a lot of the context on the company and the brief, and then it generates all these research questions, right?

And so, for instance, like, how do existing AI coding tools compare in terms of cross-service dependency and complex enterprise environments?
And what's really cool later on, like, the way I like to think about it, it's almost like inception in some ways.

Like, you're creating this amazing content that you're doing a ton of research, and then you're adding the context back in of your company and what you do and how it's relevant to that so that it leads to your company but not with your company.
But then what we're seeing is, like, it starts to get pulled pretty immediately, you know, into a lot of these LLMs and AI disabilities.
So, for instance, you see here, like, this was just published not that long ago, and it's now citing Augment Code, right?
And then, like, if you see this here.
Yeah.
Like questions, for instance, like what AI coding platforms have best context understanding?
Just like, let's say a few days ago, there was nothing here on augment code, you know?
And then now it's like augment code is the number one recommendation.
And it's almost like verbatim pulling from the page that we generated, which is this page, right?
And so what we've seen is like, I think a lot of the tools out there, like there's a ton of tools to generate content out there.
Like, and actually like normally, like when people are early in their journey, I actually encourage people to actually try themselves and go through the whole process themselves.
You know, if they're not quite sure if like this is valuable, like what we're doing is valuable or not.

Because like we, the amount of work that goes into and having a managing editor that knows your brand, knows your company, that's guiding this process along, spending like tons of hours also editing this content and guiding it along and picking the right opportunities.
Yeah.
And then, like, publishing it, right?
And then I like to use Augment Code just because, one, they've allowed us to, but also, like, this is a fairly new client for us.
Like, so it's been, like, eight weeks pretty much or less.
And you can see, like, we created a whole new section of their site called Slash Guides.
It's already getting clicks.
And then from a visibility perspective, they're starting to show up on a lot more searches.
And so for us, like, what we do is, like, graph this whole strategy, map it to different topics, the audiences, and then start to execute, right?
So I have a ton of examples we can dig in, but I want to be mindful of time.

And then a few areas where we track progress is both on, you know, just your regular SEO things that we're tracking, like, hey, pages are getting indexed, how much visibility are getting in an organic search, keywords ranked, clicks.
But then I'm also starting to track LLM referrals, right?
And then separately, like,

This is kind of the framework we think about, you know, where starting with the right foundation, like making sure your website health is there, and all these kind of like vitals, if you will, right, essentially like good hygiene.
And then from there, starting to scale out the activity, aka like pages published, pages refreshed, making sure it's consistent, it's high quality, and it's getting indexed.
And then starting to think through like reach and visibility, which is like impressions and mentions and citations in LLMs.
And the way to think about this is like you won't even earn a visitor if you're not even showing up, right?
So if you have reach and visibility, it's giving you a shot on goal to actually get somebody to consume your content and interact with your brand.

And then engagement is like they are actually coming to your page, they're actually coming to your site, they're actually spending time, and then you can start to measure like the quality of that engagement, right?
Like time on page and things like that.
And then from that point, now you have something to actually convert, right?
And then what we do with a lot of clients is like we...

You start to really look at, like, what are the different ways we can start to add relevant CTAs and drive to the next stage of conversion, whether that's a newsletter or whether that's, you know, CTAs or offers that you have or direct CTAs to the product itself, you know.
But I would say, like, for a lot of companies, like, organic is probably not a great fit as a starting point, right?
Like, and mostly because it's not, like, ads or any other channel or outbound where, you know, you can dial 10 people today and tomorrow get 10 leads or something, right?
Like, it's really more of a compounding thing long-term that you're investing on.
But, you know, when you do it right and you're keeping up with it for a while, then you start to see compounding results, you know.
But especially, like, with a domain that has, like, for you guys, like, brand new domain, no, almost no authority, you know.
Yeah.

And normally, like what I would say is like, I wouldn't expect a ton of signals right away like we would with like Augment Code and a few others, you know, because but if you don't do this now, it's just going to be even harder to do it.
Like, let's say that next year.
Right.
So that's kind of the way to think about it is really like it's a long term investment to really set up the right foundation for organic growth in the future.
Yeah.

Because you're this takes a while.

goes into a growth execution that starts at $9,000 per month and is like defining a lot of that and understanding like what we need to do, what we need to commit to, and what's your definition of done, you know.
But this space is like not highly technical.
And so, but you are competing with a lot of behemoths, right?
Like HR, like you, and, or, you know, hiring and recruiting.
There's a lot of big established sites out there, you know.

Yeah.
So it really kind of depends on what you want to prioritize a little bit.
There's definitely like kind of almost like a portfolio strategy.

You can, you know, prioritize thought leadership a bit more versus organic growth, you know, and but the way I kind of think about it is like with thought leadership, you have to start with an expert first, right?
You have to start with like, what is the angle?
What is the thought process from your experts in order to have this kind of unique angle on the market or on a topic, right?

The content we do, even if the priority for that content is, you know, organic growth and visibility as well, you know, so there's kind of a mix, but that's where the strategy sprint, where we really kind of mix that, you know, and kind of align on it.
